वरुथरैचा करी कुज़म्बू परोटा के साथ

Freshly Ground Spice Curry with Layered Flatbread
hindi: वरुथरैचा करी कुज़म्बू परोटा के साथ

food_subgroup: Curry with flatbread — South Indian spiced meat or vegetable curry served with layered parotta

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu with Parotta. A0FJP (curry, meat-based, with bread) is the closest defensible parent. Parotta is a layered flatbread analogous to bread in this context.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific South Indian curry-flatbread combination.

main_ingredients: freshly roasted spices (coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, dried red chillies)

serving.description: 1 serving: curry portion with 2 pieces of parotta
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving consists of approximately 250 g of Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu (curry) accompanied by 2 pieces of parotta (approximately 100 g each, total 200 g), giving a combined serving weight of approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN; composite estimate for mutton curry (roasted spice) and parotta
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu with Parotta as a combined dish. Values are estimated by weighted composition: approximately 56% curry (mutton/chicken curry with roasted spice base, ICMR-NIN entries for meat curry and coconut-based gravies) and 44% parotta (ICMR-NIN entry for parotta/layered flatbread). Per-100g values are derived from this weighted blend. Energy cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entries for analogous South Asian curry and flatbread components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on parotta (unleavened refined wheat flatbread, GI ~62) and South Indian meat curry (low GI contribution)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu with Parotta. Parotta (refined wheat layered flatbread) is the dominant glycemic driver. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values of approximately 57–67 for refined wheat flatbreads (chapati/parotta analogues). A composite GI of 62 is assigned, weighted by carbohydrate contribution: parotta (~44% of serving weight, ~85% of total carbohydrate) dominates; the curry component (coconut, meat, spices) contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per serving = (62 × 82.8 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 51.3, rounded to 51.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~390 mg/100g; ~1755 mg per full serving) from spice blends, salt in curry, and parotta preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced salt preparation, as a full serving provides approximately 74% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (GI 62) driven by refined wheat parotta; high carbohydrate load per serving (~82.8 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this meal to a half-portion or substitute parotta with a lower-GI whole-grain bread, as the full serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 51, which is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content (~4.1 g/100g) from coconut and meat; high sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, as the combination of saturated fat from roasted coconut and meat, together with high sodium, may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu with Parotta is a traditional South Indian meal originating from Tamil Nadu, consisting of two distinct components: a richly spiced meat curry and a flaky layered flatbread. 'Varutharaicha' (Tamil: வறுத்தரைச்ச) means 'roasted and ground', referring to the technique of dry-roasting whole spices — typically coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, dried red chillies, and grated coconut — before grinding them into a fresh masala paste that forms the flavour base of the curry. The curry (kuzhambu) is slow-simmered with onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and curry leaves, producing a deeply aromatic, thick, dark-brown gravy with complex smoky and earthy notes. The protein component is typically mutton or chicken. Parotta is a multi-layered, flaky flatbread made from refined wheat flour (maida), kneaded with oil or ghee, coiled and flattened repeatedly to create distinct laminated layers, then cooked on a hot griddle. The combination is a staple restaurant and street-food meal across Tamil Nadu and is widely served in South Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~198 kcal/100g), providing moderate protein (~9.2 g/100g) from meat, significant carbohydrate (~18.4 g/100g) from parotta, and moderate fat (~10.5 g/100g) from coconut, oil, and meat. Sodium is high (~390 mg/100g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), driven primarily by the refined wheat parotta, and the glycemic load per full serving is approximately 51, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally dairy-free and halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified meat.

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu with Parotta as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component entries for South Indian meat curry and parotta, weighted by approximate serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flatbread GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are authoritative and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN entries for South Indian meat curry (roasted spice base) and parotta, weighted at approximately 56% curry and 44% parotta by mass. No single approved-source entry covers this exact combined dish. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these food categories. Selenium is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these food categories. 3. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu with Parotta was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on parotta (refined wheat flatbread) GI values from Atkinson et al. (2021) and the University of Sydney GI Database, with the curry component treated as negligible glycemic contributor. 4. Serving size of 450 g (250 g curry + 2 × 100 g parotta) is based on typical South Indian restaurant portioning and the input record description of '2 Pieces' of parotta. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. The monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g values are estimated from ICMR-NIN fatty acid profiles for analogous curry and parotta components; these carry lower confidence than the primary macros.
